title: The Comeback of Vinyl Records
summary: A Brooklyn record-store owner narrates why vinyl has returned, what kind of customer is actually buying it, and why the revival is more fragile than it looks.
body: If you had walked into my shop in 2008, you would have seen empty bins and three loyal customers arguing about jazz pressings. I was about to close. Vinyl was supposed to be dead — the CD had already replaced it, and streaming was about to replace the CD. Then something strange happened. By 2015, vinyl had started to sell again, slowly at first, then faster than anyone in the industry had predicted. Last year, sales of new vinyl in this country were higher than they had been since 1988. From the outside, this looks like a clean victory for a beautiful old format. Inside the shop, the story is more complicated. The first surprise is who is buying. People assume it's old men replacing their lost collections, but most of my customers are in their twenties. Many of them have never owned a CD player, and a few have arrived asking how the needle works. They are buying vinyl not out of nostalgia but because it feels like the opposite of streaming — slow, physical, deliberate. You cannot scroll past a record once you've put it on. The second surprise is what they're buying. New pressings from current artists outsell classic reissues by a wide margin. A Taylor Swift album on coloured vinyl can move more units in a week than any Miles Davis record I've stocked all year. This has been good for my business, but it has changed the meaning of the shop. I no longer feel like a curator; I feel more like a small retailer of merchandise. The third surprise is that the revival is more fragile than it looks. Pressing plants have not been built fast enough to match demand. Vinyl now takes six to nine months to manufacture, and prices have risen sharply. A new record that cost fifteen pounds five years ago now costs thirty. At some point, a new generation will decide that thirty pounds for a single album is not worth it, and the line on the chart will turn downwards again. None of this should stop you from buying vinyl, if that's what you enjoy. The sound is warmer than people who only listen to streaming will ever realise, and the ritual of choosing a record and placing the needle changes the way you pay attention. But if you are buying for an investment, please don't. If you are buying because you've been told it's better, listen to the same album on three formats and decide for yourself. Vinyl is not magic. It is one excellent way among several to experience music, and the people who love it most will tell you that openly. The rest is marketing, and the marketing is what worries me about how long this comeback will last.

annotations.exampleEn: Even in lean years, his loyal customers kept the shop alive.
Original first pressings are highly valued by collectors.
Cassettes were supposed to be dead, yet sales have crept up again.
No one had predicted that vinyl would outsell CDs again.
He bought the album out of nostalgia, not because he still liked the music.

annotations.meaningVi: có chủ ý, thong thả có cân nhắc
annotations.exampleEn: Listening to vinyl is slower and more deliberate than streaming.
It's too easy to scroll past a song on a streaming app.

annotations.meaningVi: đĩa than màu (phiên bản đặc biệt)
annotations.exampleEn: Coloured vinyl editions sell out within hours.
The home team won by a wide margin.

annotations.meaningVi: người chọn lọc, người phụ trách (bộ sưu tập, không gian)
annotations.exampleEn: A good record-shop owner is more curator than salesperson.
Concerts now make more money from merchandise than from tickets.
There aren't enough pressing plants to meet today's vinyl demand.

annotations.phrase: the line on the chart will turn downwards
annotations.meaningVi: đường biểu đồ sẽ đi xuống
annotations.pronunciation: /ðə laɪn ɒn ðə tʃɑːt wɪl tɜːn ˈdaʊnwədz/
annotations.exampleEn: Once prices get too high, the line on the chart will turn downwards.

annotations.meaningVi: nghi thức, thói quen mang tính nghi thức
annotations.exampleEn: There's a ritual to choosing a record that streaming has erased.

annotations.meaningVi: phần còn lại chỉ là chiêu tiếp thị
annotations.exampleEn: Vinyl genuinely sounds different. The rest is marketing.

grammarNotes.title: Past Perfect for Earlier Events in a Story
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn dùng thì quá khứ hoàn thành để chỉ những hành động xảy ra trước một mốc quá khứ khác: 'By 2015, vinyl had started to sell again', 'sales were higher than they had been since 1988', 'faster than anyone in the industry had predicted'. Cấu trúc: had + V3. Dùng khi muốn nhấn mạnh một sự kiện xảy ra trước một sự kiện quá khứ khác, thường kết hợp với 'by + thời gian' hoặc 'than'.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage uses the past perfect to mark earlier events in a past narrative: 'By 2015, vinyl had started to sell again', 'sales were higher than they had been since 1988', 'faster than anyone in the industry had predicted'. Form: had + past participle. Use to mark an event earlier than another past event — often paired with 'by + time' or 'than'.

grammarNotes.title: Third Conditional and Modal Speculation
grammarNotes.bodyVi: Đoạn văn mở đầu bằng câu điều kiện loại 3: 'If you had walked into my shop in 2008, you would have seen empty bins'. Và đoạn văn dùng các động từ khuyết thiếu để suy đoán: 'a new generation will decide', 'people who only listen to streaming will ever realise'. Loại 3 dùng để nói về tình huống giả định trong quá khứ; 'will' dùng để dự đoán có cơ sở về tương lai.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: The passage opens with a third conditional: 'If you had walked into my shop in 2008, you would have seen empty bins'. It also uses modals for speculation: 'a new generation will decide', 'people who only listen to streaming will ever realise'. Third conditional describes a hypothetical past; 'will' makes confident predictions about the future.

translationVi: Nếu bạn đã bước vào cửa hàng của tôi năm 2008, bạn sẽ thấy những thùng trống và ba khách quen đang tranh cãi về các bản ép đĩa jazz. Tôi sắp đóng cửa. Đĩa than đáng lẽ phải đã chết — CD đã thay thế nó, và streaming sắp thay thế CD. Rồi điều kỳ lạ xảy ra. Đến 2015, đĩa than đã bắt đầu bán được trở lại, chậm rãi lúc đầu, rồi nhanh hơn bất cứ ai trong ngành đã dự đoán. Năm ngoái, doanh số đĩa than mới ở nước này cao hơn bất kỳ thời điểm nào kể từ 1988. Nhìn từ bên ngoài, đây có vẻ là chiến thắng rõ ràng cho một định dạng cũ đẹp đẽ. Bên trong cửa hàng, câu chuyện phức tạp hơn. Bất ngờ đầu tiên là ai đang mua. Người ta tưởng là những ông già thay thế bộ sưu tập đã mất, nhưng phần lớn khách của tôi ở tuổi hai mươi. Nhiều người chưa từng sở hữu máy CD, và vài người đến hỏi kim đĩa hoạt động ra sao. Họ mua đĩa than không phải vì hoài niệm mà vì nó cảm giác như đối lập với streaming — chậm, vật chất, có chủ ý. Bạn không thể lướt qua một đĩa khi đã đặt nó lên. Bất ngờ thứ hai là họ mua gì. Đĩa mới ép từ các nghệ sĩ đương đại bán chạy hơn hẳn các bản tái phát hành cổ điển với khoảng cách lớn. Một album Taylor Swift trên đĩa than màu có thể bán nhiều đơn vị trong một tuần hơn bất kỳ đĩa Miles Davis nào tôi đã trữ cả năm. Điều này tốt cho việc kinh doanh, nhưng đã thay đổi ý nghĩa của cửa hàng. Tôi không còn cảm thấy như người chọn lọc; tôi cảm thấy như một người bán lẻ hàng hoá nhỏ. Bất ngờ thứ ba là sự hồi sinh này mong manh hơn vẻ ngoài. Nhà máy ép đĩa không được xây đủ nhanh để đáp ứng nhu cầu. Đĩa than giờ mất sáu đến chín tháng để sản xuất, và giá đã tăng mạnh. Một đĩa mới giá mười lăm bảng năm năm trước giờ ba mươi bảng. Đến lúc nào đó, một thế hệ mới sẽ quyết định ba mươi bảng cho một album không đáng, và đường trên biểu đồ sẽ đi xuống. Tất cả điều này không nên ngăn bạn mua đĩa than, nếu bạn thích. Âm thanh ấm hơn những gì người chỉ nghe streaming sẽ bao giờ nhận ra, và nghi thức chọn đĩa và đặt kim thay đổi cách bạn chú ý. Nhưng nếu bạn mua như một khoản đầu tư, làm ơn đừng. Nếu bạn mua vì bạn được bảo nó tốt hơn, nghe cùng album trên ba định dạng và tự quyết định. Đĩa than không phải phép màu. Nó là một cách tuyệt vời trong nhiều cách để trải nghiệm âm nhạc, và những người yêu nó nhất sẽ nói với bạn điều đó một cách thẳng thắn. Phần còn lại chỉ là tiếp thị, và chính tiếp thị là điều khiến tôi lo về việc sự hồi sinh này sẽ kéo dài bao lâu.

questions.prompt: What was the state of the narrator's shop in 2008?
options: About to close, with empty bins and three loyal customers
A new branch had just opened
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'you would have seen empty bins and three loyal customers... I was about to close.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes the shop with two specific details.

questions.prompt: When did vinyl start to sell again, according to the narrator?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'By 2015, vinyl had started to sell again.'
questions.hint: A specific year is mentioned as the turning point.

questions.prompt: How does last year's vinyl sales compare historically?
options: Lower than at any previous point
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Last year, sales of new vinyl in this country were higher than they had been since 1988.'
questions.hint: A specific year of comparison is given.

questions.prompt: Who is buying most of the vinyl, according to the narrator?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'most of my customers are in their twenties.'
questions.hint: The narrator overturns a common assumption about who buys.

questions.prompt: Why are these younger buyers drawn to vinyl?
options: They believe it sounds better than every other format
Because it feels like the opposite of streaming — slow, physical, deliberate
Because it's cheaper than streaming subscriptions
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'They are buying vinyl not out of nostalgia but because it feels like the opposite of streaming — slow, physical, deliberate.'
questions.hint: The narrator contrasts vinyl's qualities with streaming.

questions.prompt: What outsells classic reissues by a wide margin?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'New pressings from current artists outsell classic reissues by a wide margin.'
questions.hint: The narrator contrasts new music with reissued older music.

questions.prompt: How does the narrator describe how their professional role has changed?
options: They feel more like a curator than ever
They feel less like a curator and more like a small retailer of merchandise
They feel like a music journalist
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'I no longer feel like a curator; I feel more like a small retailer of merchandise.'
questions.hint: The narrator describes a shift away from a specialist role.

questions.prompt: How long does vinyl now take to manufacture?
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'Vinyl now takes six to nine months to manufacture.'

questions.prompt: How have prices for new records changed in five years?
options: They have doubled from fifteen to thirty pounds
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'A new record that cost fifteen pounds five years ago now costs thirty.'

questions.prompt: What does the narrator say about buying vinyl as an investment?
options: It's an excellent way to make money
It's only worth it for jazz records
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'if you are buying for an investment, please don't.'
questions.hint: The narrator is direct on this point.

questions.prompt: What does the narrator recommend if someone has been told vinyl is 'better'?
options: Trust the experts and buy a record
Listen to the same album on three formats and decide for themselves
questions.explanation: The narrator says 'listen to the same album on three formats and decide for yourself.'

questions.prompt: What worries the narrator about how long the comeback will last?
options: Vinyl artists running out of ideas
questions.explanation: The narrator concludes 'The rest is marketing, and the marketing is what worries me.'
questions.hint: It's a concern about how vinyl is being sold, not the format itself.

questions.prompt: What is the narrator's overall stance on the vinyl revival?
options: Fully enthusiastic — vinyl will keep growing forever
Hostile — they want CDs to return
Sympathetic but realistic — they love vinyl but warn the revival is fragile and the hype is overdone
Indifferent — they only care about profit
questions.explanation: The narrator praises vinyl's sound and ritual but flags rising prices, manufacturing limits, and marketing hype as reasons the revival may not last.
questions.hint: Combine the warm praise with the warnings about fragility and marketing.

People assume it's old men replacing their lost collections, but most of my customers are in their {{b4}}. They are buying vinyl not out of {{b5}} but because it feels like the opposite of streaming — slow, physical, deliberate. The second surprise is what they're buying. New pressings from current artists outsell classic reissues by a wide {{b6}}. I no longer feel like a {{b7}}; I feel more like a small retailer of merchandise. The third surprise is that the revival is more fragile than it looks. Pressing plants have not been built fast enough. Vinyl now takes six to nine months to {{b8}}, and prices have risen sharply. A new record that cost fifteen pounds five years ago now costs thirty. If you are buying for an {{b9}}, please don't. Vinyl is not magic. The rest is {{b10}}, and the marketing is what worries me about how long this comeback will last.

blanks.explanation: 'Loyal customers' is the fixed phrase — customers who keep returning even when business is hard.
The exact word the narrator uses — 'vinyl was supposed to be dead' is the standard expression about a format's demise.
'Predicted' fits because the narrator is comparing actual sales growth with industry forecasts.
The narrator says 'most of my customers are in their twenties' — the exact age range.
The narrator specifically contrasts 'not out of nostalgia' with the appeal of slowness and physicality.
'By a wide margin' is the fixed idiom for winning by a large amount in a competition or comparison.
'Curator' is the exact noun the narrator uses — someone who selects and contextualises items.
'Manufacture' is the exact verb used — it's the standard industrial term.
'As an investment' is the fixed phrase — buying with the hope the item will rise in value.
'The rest is marketing' is the exact phrase the narrator uses to dismiss the hype around vinyl.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The narrator says the ritual of choosing a record changes how you pay attention. Are there other slow, physical experiences in your life that you would not want to be replaced by faster digital versions, and what is at stake when they disappear?
